tsx vs ts-node vs tsc for running TypeScript
body:
Three approaches to execute TypeScript, with different tradeoffs.

```bash
# tsx — fastest for development. Uses esbuild under the hood.
# No tsconfig needed. Just works.
npx tsx src/index.ts
npx tsx --watch src/index.ts   # watch mode with auto-restart
npx tsx --inspect src/index.ts # debugger support

# ts-node — mature, full type checking optional.
# Slower startup, more config needed.
npx ts-node --esm src/index.ts
# Needs tsconfig: { "ts-node": { "esm": true } }
# For ESM projects, often finicky.

# tsc — compile first, then run plain JS.
tsc && node dist/index.js
tsc --watch  # in one terminal
node --watch dist/index.js  # in another (Node 18+)
```

Recommendation:
- **Development scripts**: `tsx` — zero config, fast, handles ESM/CJS
- **Production**: `tsc` to compile, then `node dist/` — no runtime overhead
- **Testing**: most test runners (vitest, jest with ts-jest) have built-in TS support

tsx doesn't type-check — it strips types like esbuild. Run `tsc --noEmit` in CI for type safety. ts-node can type-check but is slower and often has ESM issues.

title: Vite library mode for building TypeScript packages

Node.js --inspect flag for debugging with Chrome DevTools
body:
The `--inspect` flag starts a WebSocket debugging server. Connect Chrome DevTools or VS Code to set breakpoints, profile memory, and step through code.

```bash
# Start with debugger listening
node --inspect src/index.js
# Debugger listening on ws://127.0.0.1:9229/...

# Break on first line
node --inspect-brk src/index.js

# With tsx (TypeScript)
tsx --inspect src/index.ts

# Custom port
node --inspect=0.0.0.0:9230 src/index.js
```

Connect:
1. Open `chrome://inspect` in Chrome
2. Click "Open dedicated DevTools for Node"
3. Or in VS Code, use this launch.json:

```jsonc
{
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "configurations": [{
    "type": "node",
    "request": "attach",
    "name": "Attach to Node",
    "port": 9229,
    "restart": true,
    "sourceMaps": true,
    "resolveSourceMapLocations": ["${workspaceFolder}/**", "!**/node_modules/**"]
  }]
}
```

For production debugging: use `--inspect=0.0.0.0:9229` and SSH tunnel (`ssh -L 9229:localhost:9229 server`). NEVER expose the inspect port publicly — it allows arbitrary code execution.

npm lockfile merge conflicts — safe resolution strategy
body:
package-lock.json merge conflicts are common in teams. NEVER manually edit the lockfile — let npm regenerate it.

```bash
# When you have merge conflicts in package-lock.json:

# Step 1: Accept EITHER version of the lockfile (doesn't matter which)
git checkout --theirs package-lock.json
# or: git checkout --ours package-lock.json

# Step 2: Ensure package.json is correctly merged (manually resolve if needed)
git diff package.json  # verify the merge looks right

# Step 3: Regenerate the lockfile
npm install

# Step 4: Stage both files
git add package.json package-lock.json
git commit
```

```bash
# Prevention: use npm's built-in merge driver
git config merge.npm.driver "npx npm-merge-driver merge %A %O %B %P"
# Add to .gitattributes:
echo 'package-lock.json merge=npm' >> .gitattributes
```

Why you can't manually merge: the lockfile contains integrity hashes, resolved URLs, and dependency trees that are internally consistent. Partial merges create corrupt state. Always regenerate from package.json.

title: Peer dependency warnings and resolution in npm
Publishing a TypeScript package to npm
body:
Step-by-step guide to publishing a well-configured TypeScript package.

```jsonc
// package.json
{
  "name": "@your-scope/my-lib",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "main": "./dist/index.cjs",
  "module": "./dist/index.js",
  "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "import": { "types": "./dist/index.d.ts", "default": "./dist/index.js" },
      "require": { "types": "./dist/index.d.cts", "default": "./dist/index.cjs" }
    }
  },
  "files": ["dist"],
  "scripts": {
    "build": "tsup src/index.ts --format esm,cjs --dts",
    "prepublishOnly": "npm run build",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"
  },
  "devDependencies": { "tsup": "^8.0.0", "typescript": "^5.4.0" }
}
```

```bash
# Check what will be published
npm pack --dry-run

# Publish (first time with scope)
npm publish --access public

# Publish with 2FA
npm publish --otp=123456

# Pre-release
npm version prerelease --preid=beta
npm publish --tag beta
```

Checklist: `files` field limits what's uploaded (never publish src/, tests/, .env). `prepublishOnly` ensures fresh build. Test locally with `npm link` or `npm pack` + `npm install ./my-lib-1.0.0.tgz`.

How to set up tsc --watch with nodemon for auto-restart
body:
Running `tsc --watch` compiles on change but doesn't restart the server. Combine it with `nodemon` or Node's built-in `--watch`.

```bash
# Option 1: Node 18+ built-in watch (simplest)
# Two terminals:
tsc --watch               # Terminal 1
node --watch dist/index.js # Terminal 2

# Option 2: nodemon watching dist/
npx nodemon --watch dist/ --exec 'node dist/index.js'

# Option 3: concurrently (single command)
npx concurrently \
  'tsc --watch --preserveWatchOutput' \
  'nodemon --watch dist/ -e js --exec node dist/index.js'
```

```jsonc
// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "concurrently \"tsc -w\" \"nodemon dist/index.js\"",
    "dev:simple": "tsx --watch src/index.ts"
  }
}
```

```jsonc
// nodemon.json
{
  "watch": ["dist"],
  "ext": "js,json",
  "delay": 500,
  "ignore": ["dist/**/*.map"]
}
```

Simplest approach: skip `tsc --watch` entirely and use `tsx --watch src/index.ts`. tsx transpiles TypeScript on the fly with esbuild speed. For production, still compile with `tsc` to catch type errors.

TypeScript project setup for a new Node.js API in 2025
body:
Modern TypeScript + Node.js API project setup with current best practices.

```jsonc
// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es2022",
    "module": "node16",
    "moduleResolution": "node16",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src",
    "declaration": true,
    "sourceMap": true,
    "verbatimModuleSyntax": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}
```

```jsonc
// package.json
{
  "type": "module",
  "engines": { "node": ">=20" },
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "tsx --watch src/index.ts",
    "build": "tsc",
    "start": "node dist/index.js",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    "lint": "eslint src/",
    "test": "vitest"
  }
}
```

```bash
# Install
npm install typescript tsx @types/node -D
npm install vitest -D
npm install eslint @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin -D
```

Key choices: `module: "node16"` for proper ESM support, `verbatimModuleSyntax` for explicit type imports, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` for safer object/array access. Use `tsx` for development (fast, no config), `tsc` for production builds (type-checked).

tsc --watch vs tsx --watch vs nodemon for TypeScript development
body:
Three approaches for auto-reloading TypeScript during development, with different strengths.

```bash
# tsc --watch — type-checks continuously, outputs JS
tsc --watch --preserveWatchOutput
# Pros: Full type checking, catches errors immediately
# Cons: Only compiles — need nodemon/node --watch to restart the process
# Combo: tsc --watch + node --watch dist/index.js

# tsx --watch — transpiles with esbuild, auto-restarts
npx tsx --watch src/index.ts
# Pros: Single command, instant startup (~50ms), handles ESM/CJS/paths
# Cons: NO type checking — errors only caught in IDE or CI
# Best for: API servers, scripts, rapid iteration

# nodemon with ts-node — legacy approach
npx nodemon --exec 'ts-node --esm' src/index.ts
# Pros: Configurable (delay, extensions, ignore patterns)
# Cons: Slow restart (ts-node startup), ESM issues
# Verdict: Use tsx instead
```

```jsonc
// Recommended: tsx for dev, tsc in CI
// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "tsx --watch src/index.ts",
    "build": "tsc",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    "ci": "tsc --noEmit && vitest run"
  }
}
```

The key insight: separate type-checking from transpilation. tsx gives you fast feedback loops during development. tsc catches type errors in CI before deployment.

title: How to debug Node.js performance with clinic.js flame graphs
body:
clinic.js generates flame graphs, event loop analysis, and dependency diagrams from Node.js processes.

```bash
# Install
npm install -g clinic

# Flame graph — shows where CPU time is spent
clinic flame -- node dist/server.js
# Runs the server, generates HTML flame graph on Ctrl+C
# Tall stacks = deep call chains, wide bars = CPU hot spots

# Doctor — holistic analysis (CPU, memory, event loop, I/O)
clinic doctor -- node dist/server.js
# Detects: event loop blocking, excessive GC, I/O bottlenecks

# Bubbleprof — async flow visualization
clinic bubbleprof -- node dist/server.js
# Shows time spent in async operations, reveals queue backlogs
```

```typescript
// Generate load for profiling
import autocannon from 'autocannon';

const result = await autocannon({
  url: 'http://localhost:3000/api/heavy-endpoint',
  connections: 100,
  duration: 10,
  pipelining: 1,
});
console.log(autocannon.printResult(result));
```

```bash
# Full workflow
# Terminal 1: start server under profiling
clinic flame -- node dist/server.js

# Terminal 2: generate load
npx autocannon -c 100 -d 10 http://localhost:3000/api/data

# Terminal 1: Ctrl+C → opens flame graph in browser
```

Read flame graphs bottom-up: the bottom is the entry point, each layer up is a function call. Wide plateaus at the top indicate optimization targets.
